canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, spatial reasoning is most commonly defined as the ability to understand, navigate, and comprehend relationships within 2D or 3D environments. This broad concept is also framed under more specific terms such as 'spatial understanding,' 'spatial awareness,' and 'spatial cognition,' which emphasize aspects like interpreting object positions, maintaining an internal model of location, and mentally manipulating shapes. A more detailed framing distinguishes between 'large-scale spatial cognition' for navigating environments and 'small-scale spatial cognition' for mentally transforming objects. The field operationalizes this construct using a variety of benchmarks, including VSR, RefCOCO, Maze, Bongard Problems, and GQA, which one paper notes 'validates spatial reasoning'. Several papers observe that this is a challenging capability for models; for example, one study states that 'spatial understanding appears to be the most challenging across all textual models' (ScImage: How good are multimodal large language models at scientific text-to-image generation?). The construct is frequently studied alongside visual reasoning, geometric reasoning, and referring expression understanding. Some research suggests that spatial reasoning influences downstream behaviors like robotic manipulation and pathfinding, with one paper proposing it allows models to 'generalize to novel scenarios' (Vision Language Models are In-Context Value Learners). Overall, the construct encompasses abilities ranging from interpreting simple relative positions, such as 'an orange on the left of an apple', to building and maintaining complex 3D scene representations from visual inputs over time.

definitions.text: The ability to understand and navigate within 2D or 3D environments and comprehend spatial relationships.
definitions.paper: DreamLLM: Synergistic Multimodal Comprehension and Creation

definitions.text: The ability to interpret, process, and reason about information presented through visual and spatial clues, rather than purely textual descriptions.
definitions.paper: Enhancing LLM Reasoning via Vision-Augmented Prompting

definitions.text: The model's latent ability to correctly interpret and represent spatial relationships between objects, such as their relative positions.
definitions.paper: ScImage: How good are multimodal large language models at scientific text-to-image generation?

definitions.text: The model's internal understanding of the location and spatial relationships of objects or regions within an image.
definitions.paper: Interpretable Bilingual Multimodal Large Language Model for Diverse Biomedical Tasks

definitions.text: The ability to identify and describe visual elements and their relationships within an image, such as objects or specific details.
definitions.paper: Visual Description Grounding Reduces Hallucinations and Boosts Reasoning in LVLMs

definitions.text: The model's ability to interpret the meaning of natural language expressions that describe spatial relations.
definitions.paper: Do Vision-Language Models Represent Space and How? Evaluating Spatial Frame of Reference under Ambiguities

definitions.text: The ability to understand and describe whole-image content at the scene level.
definitions.paper: ClawMachine: Learning to Fetch Visual Tokens for Referential Comprehension

definitions.text: The model's ability to interpret and understand spatial properties and relationships from visual inputs, without relying on an explicit map.
definitions.paper: ImagineNav: Prompting Vision-Language Models as Embodied Navigator through Scene Imagination

definitions.text: The ability to perceive, interpret, represent, and interact with objects and environments, including building mental representations to support navigation and manipulation.
definitions.paper: Does Spatial Cognition Emerge in Frontier Models?

definitions.text: The ability to build and use spatial representations of large environments for tasks like navigation and spatial reasoning.
definitions.paper: Does Spatial Cognition Emerge in Frontier Models?

definitions.text: The ability to perceive, imagine, and mentally transform objects or shapes in two or three dimensions.
definitions.paper: Does Spatial Cognition Emerge in Frontier Models?

definitions.text: The ability to imagine a different viewpoint in space and determine relative directions or orientations from that perspective.
definitions.paper: Does Spatial Cognition Emerge in Frontier Models?

definitions.text: The ability to mentally manipulate and transform 2D or 3D stimuli and complex spatial configurations.
definitions.paper: Does Spatial Cognition Emerge in Frontier Models?

definitions.text: The latent capacity of a model to determine the physical position of objects in an environment, particularly when reasoning from linguistic descriptions.
definitions.paper: ThinkBot: Embodied Instruction Following with Thought Chain Reasoning

definitions.text: The broader ability to understand whole-image content and preserve general vision-language performance while adding region-level prompting.
definitions.paper: Draw-and-Understand: Leveraging Visual Prompts to Enable MLLMs to Comprehend What You Want

definitions.text: The ability of a machine to perceive, reason, and act in three dimensions within space and time, encompassing the understanding of spatial relationships between objects and scenes.
definitions.paper: Where Am I and What Will I See: An Auto-Regressive Model for Spatial Localization and View Prediction

definitions.text: The a priori understanding of spatial properties and Euclidean structure in the world.
definitions.paper: Core Knowledge Deficits in Multi-Modal Language Models

definitions.text: The ability to understand and reason about directional terms and spatial orientation within a consistent reference frame.
definitions.paper: Hypo3D: Exploring Hypothetical Reasoning in 3D

definitions.text: The ability to perceive and reason about the movement, positioning, and directional changes of objects across both space and time in a video sequence.
definitions.paper: LongVU: Spatiotemporal Adaptive Compression for Long Video-Language Understanding

definitions.text: The latent ability to construct and update a mental model of the environment that integrates spatial layout and dynamic state changes over time.
definitions.paper: ConstraintLLM: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Industrial-Level Constraint Programming

definitions.text: The capacity to accumulate and synthesize geometric and visual cues across non-overlapping video frames to form a unified understanding of a scene.
definitions.paper: Promote, Suppress, Iterate: How Language Models Answer One-to-Many Factual Queries

definitions.text: The internal construction and maintenance of a metrically accurate, three-dimensional model of a scene from egocentric visual inputs, enabling spatial inference across time.
definitions.paper: Promote, Suppress, Iterate: How Language Models Answer One-to-Many Factual Queries

relationships.evidence: Our benchmark encompasses six novel hallucination scenarios ... each covering ﬁve different tasks, i.e. object recognition, counting, attribute recognition, spatial reasoning, and action prediction.

relationships.evidence: Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) (Mao et al., 2016), which involves localizing an object in an image based on a complex textual query, requiring both spatial and semantic reasoning.

relationships.paper: Imagine While Reasoning in Space: Multimodal Visualization-of-Thought
relationships.evidence: We validate MVoT’s effectiveness through controlled experiments across three dynamic spatial reasoning tasks. MAZE (Ivanitskiy et al., 2023) and MINIBEHAVIOR (Jin et al., 2023) focus on interactions with spatial layouts. (1. Introduction)

relationships.evidence: It encompasses a diverse range of question types, including object identification, spatial relationships, scene-level understanding, and general reasoning. (Section 5)

relationships.paper: Bongard in Wonderland: Visual Puzzles that Still Make AI Go Mad?
relationships.evidence: When focusing on the different BP categories (cf. Figure 4 middle), we observe that humans are substantially better at solving BPs that have spatial reasoning components. I.e., spatial is one of the trickiest categories for current VLM models, with all investigated models solving under 25%.

icml25: Bongard in Wonderland: Visual Puzzles that Still Make AI Go Mad?

relationships.paper: MultiMatch: Multihead Consistency Regularization Matching for Semi-Supervised Text Classification
relationships.evidence: In this work, we introduce TTT-Bench, a new benchmark that is designed to evaluate basic strategic, spatial, and logical reasoning abilities in LRMs through a suite of four two-player Tic-Tac-Toe-style games that humans can effortlessly solve from a young age. (Abstract)

emnlp25: MultiMatch: Multihead Consistency Regularization Matching for Semi-Supervised Text Classification

This broad concept is also framed under more specific terms such as 'spatial understanding,' 'spatial awareness,' and 'spatial cognition,' which emphasize aspects like interpreting object positions, maintaining an internal model of location, and mentally manipulating shapes.
